summary: A violent Easter weekend leaves a 16-year-old dead in Piedmont Park and a 3-year-old killed on Washington Street. Emory's basketball dream ends on a buzzer-beater, Atlanta United falls flat on 404 Day, and Decatur state legislators force the school district to reset its ECLC process.

topStory.headline: 16-Year-Old Girl Killed in Piedmont Park Shooting After 404 Day
topStory.summary: Tianah Robinson, 16, was shot dead and a 15-year-old girl was wounded near the pond in Piedmont Park Saturday night. Police say the shooting stemmed from an unpermitted gathering, not the permitted 404 Day Festival.
topStory.body:
What started as a day of citywide celebration ended in tragedy Saturday night when gunfire erupted inside Piedmont Park, killing a 16-year-old girl and wounding a 15-year-old.

The Fulton County Medical Examiner identified the victim as Tianah Robinson, 16, of Riverdale. The second victim, a 15-year-old girl shot in the shoulder, was taken to Grady Memorial Hospital in stable condition.

Atlanta police officers responded to the park just after 9 p.m. following reports of shots fired near the pond on the park's far west end. Officers found the 15-year-old first, then located Robinson, who was pronounced dead at the scene.

Investigators were quick to distinguish the shooting from the permitted 404 Day Festival, which had taken place on the opposite side of the park and concluded at 7:45 p.m. Police said the incident stemmed from an unpermitted gathering that drew a large crowd to the west end of the park after the festival wrapped.

Witnesses described a chaotic scene, with large crowds fleeing after shots rang out. K-9 units and homicide investigators processed multiple crime scene locations and recovered ballistic evidence.

The 404 Collective, Atlanta Influences Everything, ButterATL, Finish First, and Trap Music Museum, which organized the official 404 Day Weekend events, issued a statement clarifying they had no affiliation with the Piedmont Park gathering. Their events took place across multiple venues and concluded without incident.

No suspects have been identified and no arrests have been made. The shooting was one of seven gun violence incidents across Atlanta between Friday evening and Sunday afternoon, a weekend that also claimed the life of a 3-year-old child.
topStory.imageAlt: Atlanta police responding to a shooting scene

stories.headline: 3-Year-Old Dies in Easter Morning Shooting on Washington Street
stories.summary: A child was shot and killed in the 900 block of Washington Street SW early Sunday morning. Police are investigating the circumstances.
stories.body:
A 3-year-old child died at Grady Memorial Hospital after being shot in southwest Atlanta early Easter Sunday morning, marking the second gun death involving a minor in the city in less than four hours.

Atlanta police officers responded to a report of a person shot near the 900 block of Washington Street SW around 12:37 a.m. Sunday. When they arrived, they found the child with an apparent gunshot wound. The child was rushed to the hospital by ambulance but was pronounced deceased.

Neighbors at the small apartment complex expressed fear and sadness. One neighbor who declined to go on camera told reporters she was scared to see the heavy police presence and felt heartbroken for the child.

Investigators are canvassing the scene and searching the residence to determine what led to the gunfire and the exact circumstances of how the child was shot. No suspect descriptions or arrest information has been released.

The shooting occurred in the Peoplestown neighborhood, about two miles south of Piedmont Park where a 16-year-old girl had been killed just three hours earlier.
stories.imageAlt: Police scene at the 900 block of Washington Street SW in Atlanta

stories.headline: Emory Falls Two Points Short in D3 National Championship Heartbreaker
stories.summary: A last-second putback by Mary Washington's Colin Mitchell denied Emory its first national title, 75-73, after the Eagles erased a 13-point deficit in the final five minutes.
stories.body:
Emory University's best basketball season in program history ended in the cruelest possible way Sunday in Indianapolis. A buzzer-beating putback by University of Mary Washington sophomore Colin Mitchell gave UMW a 75-73 victory in the Division III national championship game at Gainbridge Fieldhouse.

Mitchell, a forward who had not started a single game all year, grabbed an offensive rebound and scored as time expired to give Mary Washington its first national title.

Emory led 30-28 at halftime in a grinding first half where both teams shot just 29%. The Eagles got a scare when national player of the year Ben Pearce left the game with an injury early in the second half. He returned but didn't hit his first field goal until late, finishing with 10 points, five rebounds, and seven assists.

Mary Washington steadily pulled ahead in the second half, building a 63-50 lead with under five minutes remaining. But Emory had one more run. The Eagles went on a 23-12 tear, with Ethan Faust (24 points, six rebounds) hitting a three-pointer to tie the game at 73 with seconds left after a UMW turnover.

The Eagles won 27 games this season under head coach Jason Zimmerman, who has been with the program for 19 years. It was their first national championship game appearance.
stories.imageAlt: Emory University men's basketball team during the Division III national championship game

stories.headline: Atlanta United Drops 3-1 to Columbus on 404 Day
stories.summary: The Five Stripes celebrated 404 Day with another frustrating home loss, falling to the Columbus Crew and dropping to 1-6-1 on the season.
stories.body:
Atlanta United's home woes continued Saturday night as the Five Stripes fell 3-1 to the Columbus Crew in front of fans at Mercedes-Benz Stadium on 404 Day.

The first half ended scoreless, though goalkeeper Lucas Hoyos was busy early, making multiple saves as Columbus controlled possession for the first 20 minutes. Atlanta actually won the first-half possession battle 55.4% to 45.6% and showed some life in the final 25 minutes before the break.

The second half was a different story. Columbus forward Wessam Abou Ali scored on a header just three minutes in, then added a second goal on a strike from outside the box at the 53-minute mark. A third Columbus goal followed before Atlanta managed its lone response in the 59th minute.

The loss drops Atlanta United to just one win in seven matches this season, continuing a stretch of disappointing results that has tested the patience of the home crowd. The team's only victory came earlier in the campaign, with a string of losses and a 0-0 draw against D.C. United on March 21 defining the home slate so far.
stories.imageAlt: Atlanta United match at Mercedes-Benz Stadium

stories.headline: Decatur Legislators Tell School Board to Reset ECLC Process
stories.summary: After passing a bill requiring voter referendums on large school bonds, Decatur's state legislators are urging the district to restart its early childhood learning center planning.
stories.body:
The Georgia General Assembly passed a local bill on April 2 that will require a voter referendum for certain City Schools of Decatur bonds exceeding $20 million. Following the bill's passage, Decatur's state legislators are urging the school district to reset its process for the Early Childhood Learning Center, a project that has drawn significant community debate.

The new law gives Decatur voters a direct say on major bond issuances, adding a layer of accountability to the district's capital planning. Legislators said the intent is to ensure community input on large-dollar projects rather than leaving bonding authority entirely with the school board.

The ECLC project has been a point of contention in Decatur, with parents, residents, and educators weighing in on everything from the proposed location to the scope and cost of the facility. By pushing for a reset, legislators are signaling that the current process needs broader community buy-in before moving forward.

The school board has not yet publicly responded to the legislative call for a reset. The bill's passage adds urgency to a debate that has defined much of Decatur's local politics over the past year.

stories.headline: Georgia State University Eliminates Gender Studies Major
stories.summary: GSU has dropped its gender studies major, joining a broader trend of program cuts at public universities across the state.
stories.body:
Georgia State University has eliminated its gender studies major, a move that comes amid broader scrutiny of academic programs at public universities across Georgia and the country.

The decision follows a pattern of program reviews and cuts at state institutions, where administrators and governing boards have increasingly questioned the enrollment numbers and workforce alignment of certain humanities and social science programs.

GSU, the largest university in Georgia by enrollment, has not issued a detailed public statement on the reasoning behind the cut. The university serves a diverse student body of more than 50,000 students at its Downtown Atlanta campus and satellite locations.

The elimination of the gender studies major at a major urban research university is likely to draw attention from both critics and defenders of such programs. Supporters argue these programs provide critical analytical skills and attract students interested in law, policy, and social services. Critics contend they draw insufficient enrollment to justify their cost.

It remains unclear how many current students are affected or whether a minor or certificate option will remain available.
stories.imageAlt: Georgia State University campus in Downtown Atlanta

stories.headline: DeKalb County DA Warns of Legal Fight if HB 369 Becomes Law
stories.summary: The DeKalb County district attorney is signaling a court challenge to HB 369, a bill that could reshape prosecutorial authority in Georgia.
stories.body:
DeKalb County's district attorney is warning that HB 369, a bill advancing through the Georgia legislature, could face a legal challenge if signed into law.

The bill, which has drawn criticism from prosecutors and civil liberties groups, would reshape aspects of prosecutorial authority in Georgia. The DeKalb DA's office has been vocal about concerns that the legislation could limit the ability of local prosecutors to exercise discretion in how they prioritize cases and allocate resources.

DeKalb County, which includes parts of Decatur, Druid Hills, and other ITP neighborhoods, has been at the center of debates over criminal justice reform and prosecutorial independence in recent years.

The DA's warning of a legal fight signals that the bill's passage would not end the political battle but rather shift it to the courts. Georgia's district attorneys have historically operated with broad discretion, and any effort to curtail that authority is likely to face constitutional scrutiny.

Governor Brian Kemp has not yet indicated whether he would sign the bill.
